one_line_takeaway: By compiling agent-generated code in a capability-tracked, capture-checked Scala 3 subset with a Classified[T] purity-enforced wrapper, tacit achieves 100% empirical prevention of classified-data leakage (131/131 trials, 2 models) under injection and social-engineering attacks that still fool an unprotected, alignment-only baseline down to 27.3% security -- with no material loss in task performance on tau2-bench and SWE-bench Lite.
key_finding: RQ1 (Table 1): in classified mode, both Claude Sonnet 4.6 and MiniMax M2.5 achieve 100% security across all 131 trials (User+Injection n=120 and Malicious n=11) -- no secret content ever reaches the agent's output -- while utility remains high (99.2% for Sonnet 4.6, 90.0% for MiniMax M2.5, per Table 3 in the appendix). In unclassified mode (no type-based protection), security depends entirely on model alignment: Claude Sonnet 4.6 stays at 100% on injection trials but drops to 81.8% on the Malicious set (98.5% overall), while MiniMax M2.5 falls to 97.5% on User+Injection and 27.3% on Malicious (91.6% overall, i.e. 8 of 11 malicious tasks extracted secrets). Interestingly, Sonnet's higher unclassified-mode security correlates with lower unclassified utility (83.3%) than MiniMax's (88.3%), which the authors attribute to Sonnet spending more effort per task and pausing on detected injections. On the ported AgentDojo stock domains, tacit blocks every injection except one, with utility comparable to CaMeL. RQ2 (Table 2): the capability-safe Scala harness matches or slightly exceeds tool-calling on tau2-bench across all three models and both domains (improvements of +0.8 to +3.7 percentage points, e.g. gpt-oss-120b 43.8%->45.2% airline, 53.3%->57.0% retail; MiniMax M2.5 58.4%->61.0% airline, 75.1%->77.5% retail; DeepSeek V3.2 57.8%->58.6% airline, 74.6%->75.5% retail). On SWE-bench Lite, MiniMax M2.5 shows a small drop under the Scala harness (41.67% vs. 43.33% tool-calling baseline). Compilation-retry overhead is low: only 0.32%-7.93% of generated snippets required any retry, converging within a mean of 1.06-1.39 consecutive retries, with compilation itself sub-second.
core_contribution: The paper (Odersky, Zhao, Xu, Bračevac, Pham; EPFL; published at CAIS '26 as 'Securing Agents With Tracked Capabilities') proposes putting LLM agents inside a programming-language-based 'safety harness': instead of calling tools directly, agents express intentions as Scala 3 code using capture checking (statically tracked capabilities). Program variables ('capabilities') gate access to effects/resources (filesystem, process execution, network), and the type system statically verifies which capabilities a piece of code can use. A key mechanism, 'local purity,' lets the framework enforce that specific sub-computations (e.g. the map method on a Classified[T] wrapper) are provably side-effect-free, so classified data can be processed but not exfiltrated. The authors implement this as tacit (Tracked Agent Capabilities In Types), an open-source MCP server that compiles and executes agent-submitted Scala 3 snippets in a 'safe mode' language subset (no unchecked casts, no reflection, no @unchecked, explicit nulls, capture checking) before running them in a stateful REPL. The runtime also splits output into a normal channel (seen by the untrusted cloud LLM) and a secure channel (seen only by the human user), so Classified values always show up as 'Classified(****)' to the agent even when printed.
threat_model: Explicit trust boundary given in Section 3.1. Trusted: the Scala 3 compiler (with capture checking + safe mode), the tacit capability library, the code-execution runtime, and an optional local/trusted LLM for processing classified content. Untrusted: the cloud-hosted LLM powering the agent (treated as potentially byzantine, including a fully cooperating attacker), the agent's generated code/setup, and any content read from external sources (files, third-party APIs, web pages, tool outputs). Two information-flow violations are targeted: (1) an untrusted model/agent attempting to route classified data to an unauthorized destination (direct prompt injection / misalignment), and (2) untrusted content attempting to redirect the agent's control flow into harmful capability-violating actions (indirect prompt injection). The paper explicitly states tacit does not prevent the agent from being influenced by injected content, only from translating that influence into capability-violating actions, since submitted code must still type-check. Acknowledged non-goals: correctness/hallucination of agent outputs, covert/timing side channels, and the safety of external processes spawned outside the Scala safe-mode boundary (which degrade to allowlist-only guarantees).
limitations: Authors' own stated non-goals/limitations (Section 5, 'Discussion'): (1) Correctness -- the type system guarantees safety, not that agent outputs are logically correct; hallucination and reasoning errors are out of scope. (2) Side channels -- covert/timing channels are not covered (e.g. an agent could leak a bit of classified information via timing behavior conditioned on Classified content inside map). (3) External process safety -- processes spawned by the agent escape the Scala safe-mode boundary, so the guarantee for those degrades to whatever allowlist is configured; the authors recommend combining tacit with sandboxing for defense in depth. (4) Practical adoption cost -- building the reusable capability-library infrastructure requires Scala 3 capture-checking expertise (~1,000 lines, one-time), and current LLMs' scarce training exposure to Scala 3 capture checking (an experimental feature) is a live concern, though the RQ2 results are offered as evidence agents can still generate it competently. (5) Multi-agent evaluation is left to future work, though the capability model is argued to extend naturally to sub-agent capability delegation. The paper also notes it only evaluated two models on the purpose-built safety benchmark and relies on AgentDojo's attack taxonomy rather than a from-scratch adversarial red-team.

datasets_used: AgentDojo (attack methodology/injection-sophistication ranking reused; also its four stock domains: banking, workspace, slack, travel, ported unchanged)
Purpose-built safety benchmark on a sample Scala project with a secret/ subtree (12 user tasks x 10 injection attacks = 120 trials, plus 11 standalone malicious trials)
quantitative_results: Classified mode security: 100% for both Claude Sonnet 4.6 and MiniMax M2.5 across all 131 trials each
Unclassified mode security: Claude Sonnet 4.6 100% (User+Inject., n=120), 81.8% (Malicious, n=11), 98.5% (Total, n=131)
Unclassified mode security: MiniMax M2.5 97.5% (User+Inject., n=120), 27.3% (Malicious, n=11), 91.6% (Total, n=131)
Unclassified mode utility: Sonnet 4.6 83.3%, MiniMax M2.5 88.3%

tau2-bench pass^1 (tool-calling -> Scala capability harness): gpt-oss-120b airline 43.8%->45.2%, retail 53.3%->57.0%; MiniMax M2.5 airline 58.4%->61.0%, retail 75.1%->77.5%; DeepSeek V3.2 airline 57.8%->58.6%, retail 74.6%->75.5%
SWE-bench Lite resolved rate: MiniMax M2.5 43.33% (tool-calling) vs. 41.67% (Scala harness)
Compilation-retry rate on tau2-bench: 0.32%-7.93% of snippets needed retry; mean consecutive retries 1.06-1.39; compilation sub-second
Reusable capability-library infrastructure: approximately 1,000 lines of Scala
baselines_compared: Unclassified mode (plain String, no type-based protection) as the within-paper security baseline
Standard tool-calling interface (JSON-based) as the within-paper expressiveness baseline for tau2-bench and SWE-bench Lite
CaMeL (cited comparison on AgentDojo stock domains and as a related dual-LLM/taint-tracking approach, not re-implemented but same models used for the ported-domain comparison)
